coredata.dc:title: A hydrogel-based chemosensor applied in conjunction with a Griess assay for real-time colorimetric detection of nitrite in the environment
coredata.dc:creator: Tai, Yu-Ting
Cheng, Chia-Yun
Chen, Yen-Shuo
Ko, Fu-Hsiang
coredata.dc:description: Nitrite ions (NO2 -) are Type A inorganic contaminants, and they exert significant effects and greatly threaten human health and the environment. In addition, excessive intake of this anion might cause harmful diseases. In this study, we have successfully developed a novel hydrogel-based chemosensor for sensing nitrite ions. We first synthesized a hydrogel, 2-naphthylacetic acid- L -phenylalanine- L -phenylalanine (Nap-FF), which was based on a naphthyl acetic moiety made by a liquid-phase synthetic strategy and then investigated its characteristics and the conditions required for hydrogelation. To detect nitrite ions affecting water quality, facile synthesis of the Nap-FF hydrogel and sulfanilamide were used. Then, based on the Griess mechanism for the reaction between NO2 - and NapFF-sulfanilamide, a colorimetric Griess-based reaction was performed on the hydrogel. The results confirmed visually that our chemosensor showed extremely high sensitivity to nitrite, and a significant color change from transparent to yellow/orange was observed. Correspondingly, a linear relationship was observed between the NO2 - concentration and the color difference value (ΔE*), the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.72 μM and the working concentration for NO2 - ranged from 8.3 μM to 16.7 mM. Furthermore, interference testing of this sensor confirmed its high selectivity toward NO2 - in the presence of environmentally relevant competing ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrite
Chemosensor
Griess
Hydrogel

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of griess test and colorimetric detection of nitrite with a hydrogel-based chemosensor
Synthesis and characterization of Nap-FF and NapFF-sulfanilamide
Colorimetric variations triggered by nitrite with traditional griess reagents
NapFF-sulfanilamide hydrogel colorimetric variations triggered by nitrite
Gelation test of chemosensors with different nitrite concentrations
Stability analysis of chemosensors with or without nitrite detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan for ﬁnancially supporting this research under contracts MOST 111–2113-M–A49–031 and MOST 110–2119-M-002–018 -MBK . This work is also supported by the Higher Education Sprout Project of the National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University and Ministry of Education, Taiwan .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectrum and chemical structure of NapFF, NapFF-sulfanilamide, and azo compound.
(A)Photograph and (B) ∆E * ab performance of Griess reagents at different nitrite concentrations by color measurement spectrophotometry.
(A) Photograph and (B) ∆E * ab performance of hydrogel-based chemosensors at different nitrite concentrations by color measurement spectrophotometry.

Gelation of hydrogel-based chemosensors with different nitrite concentrations. The concentration of nitrite from top to bottom and from left to right is in the following order: 16.7 mM, 10 mM, 5 mM, 3.3 mM, 1.7 mM, 0.8 mM, 0.3 mM, 0.17 mM, 66.7 μM, 33.3 μM, 16.7 μM, 8.3 μM, and 0 μM.

Colorimetric responses of NapFF-sulfanilamide to nitrite and different coexistence. (A) The corresponding color change and (B) the ratio of ΔE* of the sensing in different cations and nitrite (NO2 -).
Synthetic routes to (A) Nap-FF and (B) NapFF-sulfanilamide via the sulfo-NHS/EDC reaction and (C) azo compound formation.
Comparison of various analytical methods with our hydrogel-based chemosensor for the determination of nitrite ions.
Determination of NO2 - spiked into different water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean value and standard deviation of three measurements.

head.ce:title: A hydrogel-based chemosensor applied in conjunction with a Griess assay for real-time colorimetric detection of nitrite in the environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University, Hsinchu 300, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University, Hsinchu 300, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Advanced Science and Technology, The University of Tokyo, 4-6-1 Komaba, Meguro-ku, Tokyo 153-8904, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Advanced Science and Technology, The University of Tokyo

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Advanced Science and Technology, The University of Tokyo, 4-6-1 Komaba, Meguro-ku, Tokyo 153-8904, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrite ions (NO2 -) are Type A inorganic contaminants, and they exert significant effects and greatly threaten human health and the environment. In addition, excessive intake of this anion might cause harmful diseases. In this study, we have successfully developed a novel hydrogel-based chemosensor for sensing nitrite ions. We first synthesized a hydrogel, 2-naphthylacetic acid- L -phenylalanine- L -phenylalanine (Nap-FF), which was based on a naphthyl acetic moiety made by a liquid-phase synthetic strategy and then investigated its characteristics and the conditions required for hydrogelation. To detect nitrite ions affecting water quality, facile synthesis of the Nap-FF hydrogel and sulfanilamide were used. Then, based on the Griess mechanism for the reaction between NO2 - and NapFF-sulfanilamide, a colorimetric Griess-based reaction was performed on the hydrogel. The results confirmed visually that our chemosensor showed extremely high sensitivity to nitrite, and a significant color change from transparent to yellow/orange was observed. Correspondingly, a linear relationship was observed between the NO2 - concentration and the color difference value (ΔE*), the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.72 μM and the working concentration for NO2 - ranged from 8.3 μM to 16.7 mM. Furthermore, interference testing of this sensor confirmed its high selectivity toward NO2 - in the presence of environmentally relevant competing ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite (NO2 -) ions, as important inorganic anions and nitrogen-containing nutrients in the nitrogen biogeochemical cycle, have been extensively used along with NO3 - and NH4 + in fertilizers for the improvement of plant growth [1–6]. Widespread application has led to the ubiquitous distribution of nitrite (NO2 -) in the environment. In addition, NO2 - is often used in meat and fish product preservation as an additive and preservative. However, it is also acknowledged to be an important precursor upon interaction with amines in the production of N-nitrosamines [7,8], which have been identified as carcinogenic compounds, subsequently increasing the chances of cancer and malformation in humans [9–12]. Therefore, nitrite detection is a critical issue for human health and water environment management.

Various types of analytical techniques have been developed to enable sensitive and selective NO2 - detection in aquatic environments, such as electrochemical methods [13], capillary electrophoresis [14,15], fluorescence spectroscopy [16,17], and spectrophotometry [18]. Although these detection methods provide many benefits, they also have limited application in the routine detection of NO2 - due to the dependence on expensive instruments, precious reagents, skilled operators and long cultivation times. Hence, the use of these techniques for the sensitive and selective determination of NO2 - is still a significant challenge. Among these techniques, colorimetric determination has undoubtedly become the most promising method and has been widely used in practical applications, such as the detection of trace levels of NO2 -, for decades because of its many advantages, including simple operation, high cost-effectiveness, simple technology, excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, and fast response.

As to determinations of nitrite contents, some interesting methods combining metal nanoparticles or nanoclusters have been reported. Ibrahim et al. reported a silver nanoparticle system that is synthesized, stabilized and functionalized by aminobenzoic acid (PABA) and 4-amino-5-hydroxynapthalene-2,7-disulfonic acid monosodium salt (AHNDMS) [1]. When nitrite is detected, the original silver nanoparticles aggregate due to nitrite-selective diazo-coupling. Moreover, their system changed color from brownish yellow (dispersed AgNPs) to pinkish red (aggregated AgNPs) and the UV–vis spectra also showed a change in absorbance, with the absorbance disappearing at 474 nm and a new peak appearing at 532 nm. Second, other assays or mechanisms have involved synthesis by using compounds and reagents. A new probe called aza-BODIPY was fabricated by Ramaiah’s group [18]. This aza-BODIPY probe could selectively detect nitrite ions by a distinct visual color change from royal blue to intense green. Simultaneously, the absorbance results showed slight changes. The peak at 570 nm decreased and that at 630 nm gradually increased. Moreover, the most common colorimetric method for nitrite detection is called the Griess assay. The Griess assay, a famous analytical chemistry test, can detect the presence of nitrite ions in solution, including tap water, drinking water, plasma, serum, urine, saliva, and cell culture media [19]. This system is based on a chemical reaction that uses two Griess reagents, sulfanilamide and N-1-naphthylethylenediamine dihydrochloride (NED), under acidic conditions, such as in phosphoric acid. When sulfanilamide is added, NO2 - reacts with it in the Griess diazotization reaction to form an electrophilic diazonium salt. After that, the electrophilic diazonium salt reacts with NED in an azo coupling reaction to produce an azo dye, which is an organic compound bearing the functional group R−NN − R’. Until now, the Griess test has remained the most popular colorimetric assay for the detection of nitrite. Additionally, Griess-based methods are standard chemical analysis methods [20] used for determination of nitrite and were approved by the Association of Official Agricultural Chemists (AOAC) [21], European Committee for Standardization (CEN) [19], Chinese Ministry of Health (CHM) [22], and International Organization for Standardization (ISO) [23]. Therefore, we chose the Griess mechanism for our chemosensor establishment.

There are also many sensors and systems that use the mechanism of the colorimetric Griess assay to detect nitrite ions. Woollard et al. improved some of the original methods, adjusted the ratio of Griess reagents, and added vanadium chloride solution as a reducing agent. Hence, both nitrate and nitrite could be detected in milk and milk powders. By using a spectrophotometer, they obtained the absorbance of samples and plotted standard graphs [24]. Kunduru et al. synthesized a polymer with Griess reagents. These polymers were prepared as film sensors to detect nitrate ions in urea [25]. As a consequence, we know that development of the mechanism for the Griess assay is mature, and the assay has been applied with many forms of sensors. To observe the color change obviously, three-dimensional hydrogels was used for our substrate.

Hydrogels can be divided into different types depending on their compositions and molecular interactions. Macromolecular hydrogels and supramolecular hydrogels are the two main classifications. Macromolecular hydrogels are also called polymeric hydrogels; they have been known for centuries and are formed by strong chemical bonds. However, the interactions of polymeric hydrogels are too strong, so they cannot be re-dissolved and formation is thermally irreversible. On the other hand, supramolecular hydrogels are composed of well-designed amphiphilic low-molecular weight compounds which interact non-covalently with one another and are able to self-assemble and re-dissolve in solvents [26]. These hydrogels show more potential and should be more useful than polymeric hydrogels because of their characteristics, such as facile preparation without polymerization, effective introduction of functional groups, and reversible transformations [27]. Lately, applications of hydrogels such as agarose [28], acrylamides [29,30], methacrylamides [31], and polysaccharides [32] were described. Supramolecular hydrogels were also widely used in tissue engineering [33,34], bioanalysis [35], drug delivery [106], and preparation of chemical sensors [36]. For example, Jihye et al. produced a PEG hydrogel-based sensor with the Griess assay for nitrite detection [37]. Although some researchers have sought to apply polymeric hydrogels in biomedical fields, there are still many limitations [38] arising especially from the cytotoxicities of polymers that cannot be controlled by our immune system. To overcome this limitation, hydrogels based on peptides have been synthesized. A well-known example is the diphenylalanine peptide (FF); this is a bio-inspired peptide extracted from the β-amyloid peptide related to Alzheimer's disease, and it serves as the core recognition motif for molecular self-assembly. By combining Fmoc as a capping group, Fmoc-FF can rapidly induced hydrogelation and has been applied in several applications such as biosensing platforms and templates. For instance, Kim et al. reported a self-assembled peptide hydrogel made of Fmoc- diphenylalanine peptides by incorporating QDs (CdTe and CdSe quantum dots) and enzyme bioreceptors (glucose oxidase or horseradish peroxidase) for optical detection of analytes such as glucose or phenolic compounds. Because of the three-dimensional nanofiber matrix containing micropores, QDs and enzymes are firmly retained with minimal leakage, and the PL characteristics are maintained. Therefore, these results show that self-assembled peptide hydrogels have potential as versatile platforms for bio-sensing [39].

In this study, we propose sulfanilamide as our probe and connected it with Nap-FF to form a hydrogel through π-π interactions. With numerous advantages of low cost, facile synthesis, good selectivity, high mobility and high sensitivity, our hydrogel-based chemosensor provides a novel system for the determination of nitrite. With the 3D structure of the hydrogel, the pigment molecules can be evenly distributed throughout the hydrogel space to enable visualization of color changes. Therefore, the color rendering effect will be better and more clearly distinguishable by the naked eye. Moreover, a portable spectrophotometer has been used for quantification of color changes. Compared to that with a traditional spectrophotometer, the color analysis will be more convenient.

Acetone (C3H6O), hydrogen chloride (HCl), and dimethyl sulfoxide-d6 (DMSO-d6) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Chloroform (CHCl3) was purchased from J.T.Baker (Phillipsburg, NJ, USA). L-Phenylalanine (F) and sulfanilamide were purchased from Across Organics. 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), 2-naphthylacetic acid (Nap), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (sulfo-NHS), N, N-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), N-(1-naphthyl)ethylenediamine (NED), and sodium nitrite (NaNO2) were purchased from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA). Phosphoric acid (H3PO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) were purchased from Showa Chemical Co. (Japan). Detailed syntheses of the supramolecular hydrogel compound Nap-FF and the NapFF-sulfanilamide hydrogel materials are described in the Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of griess test and colorimetric detection of nitrite with a hydrogel-based chemosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Here, 6.6 mg of NED compound was dissolved in 20 mL of DI water to form 0.033% NED solution as the first Griess reagent. Then, 85% phosphoric acid was diluted to 5%, and 172.2 mg of sulfanilamide was dissolved in 50 mL of 5% phosphoric acid to form a 0.33% sulfanilamide solution as the second Griess reagent. Finally, different concentrations of NaNO2 solution were prepared, including 0 µM, 8.3 µM, 16.7 µM, 33.3 µM, 66.7 µM, 0.17 mM, 0.3 mM, 0.8 mM, 1.7 mM, 3.3 mM, 5 mM, 10 mM, and 16.7 mM in DI water. One milliliter of the first Griess reagent and 1 mL of the second Griess reagent were reacted for 10 min. Finally, 1 mL of different concentrations of NaNO2 solution were added, and the final azo product was formed.

To detect nitrite with the NapFF-sulfanilamide hydrogel, 1 mL of 0.033% NED solution was added to 1 mL of NapFF-sulfanilamide hydrogel and incubated for 10 min. Then, 1 mL of different concentrations of NaNO2 solution was added to the mixture separately. Finally, an azo hydrogel was formed and could detect nitrite because of the color change that occurred within 1 min. After the Griess reaction, the same volume of every azo hydrogel was added to a glass Petri dish with a diameter of 3 cm, and black flannel was stuck to the outer lid of the Petri dish. The azo hydrogel of 0 mM nitrite was set as a standard value, and then other azo hydrogels with different nitrite concentrations were detected. The color difference of the azo hydrogel was measured from the bottom of the Petri dish by using a portable color measurement spectrophotometer (HunterLab, MiniScan EZ 4500). At the same time, in addition to the colorimetric ∆ E, ∆L, ∆a, and ∆b sets of color difference data, the instrument also outputs other detected values, including three sets of CIE L, a, and b data. According to the color difference formula below, we can obtain the visual perception change of two given colors, namely, the value of ∆E*ab. The formula expresses colors with three values: L* for the lightness from black (0) to white (100), a* from green (−) to red (+), and b* from blue (−) to yellow (+):
ΔL* (brightness difference) = L2*− L1*, Δa* (red and green difference) = a2*− a1*, and Δb* (yellow and blue difference) =b2*− b1*.

To verify the selectivity of the NapFF-sulfanilamide hydrogel, potentially competing ions (cations and anions) of nitrite (NO2 -), namely, sulfate (SO4 -2), sodium (Na+), chloride (Cl-), fluoride (F-), carbonate (CO3 -2), acetate (CH3COO-), bromine (Br-), thiosulfate (S2O3 -2), oxalate (C2O4 -2), dihydrogen phosphate (H2PO4 -), ammonium (NH4 +), magnesium (Mg+2), and calcium (Ca+2) were tested with the recommended method. Nitrite and the other ions were all prepared with final concentrations of 2 mM to study the effect on our chemosensor.

Water samples were collected in the laboratory and from the bamboo lake in National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University. The samples were filtered through a 0.22-μm syringe filter, centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min with a desktop centrifuge and diluted 50 times to fit the range of our device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Nap-FF and NapFF-sulfanilamide
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, Nap-FF and NapFF-sulfanilamide were synthesized as shown in Scheme 1. To achieve the best observation of NapFF-sulfanilamide, various factors were optimized. To ensure a successful reaction, the chemical structure and FTIR spectrum of the azo compounds were compared with those of Nap-FF and NapFF-sulfanilamide in Fig. 1. The N-H stretching vibration (3340 cm-1) was attributed to the secondary amine group from the peptide moieties. The O-H stretching vibration (3500 cm-1) was attributed to the carboxyl group. Because O-H stretching and N-H stretching result in strong and broad peaks at absorption wavelengths from 3000 to 3700 cm-1, the overlapping broad peak can be ascribed to these vibrations. The stretching vibration of CO bonds (1650 cm-1) corresponds to the secondary amide group, and the peak at 1625 cm-1 can be attributed to the CC stretching vibration from conjugated alkenes. The peaks at 3070 cm-1 can be assigned to C–H stretching from aromatic groups, and C–H bending corresponds to alkyl (methyl) groups. Nap-FF, NapFF-sulfanilamide and azo compounds showed the same functional groups mentioned above. Moreover, both NapFF-sulfanilamide and azo compounds gave rise to peaks in the range of 1100–1200 cm-1 that can be assigned to SO stretching. However, there is one difference in the spectrum of azo compounds. The band at 1498–1600 cm-1, corresponding to the structural NN group, indicated that the reaction was successful [40–42]. NMR spectroscopy was also used for analysis of the compound structure, and the detailed identification data are explained in the supplementary file.

To prepare a hydrogel-based system, we designed the following experiment. Different weights of Nap-FF compound were prepared in solution. We prepared different concentrations of Nap-FF, including 0.5, 0.6, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, and 1.0 wt%, and observed their hydrogelated state. As shown in Fig. S3, the concentrations including or above 0.7 wt% remained stable in the gel state in the photos. At the same time, the strength of the hydrogel was checked by rheological testing. With the highest stability and strength of the hydrogel, the final selection of 1 wt% Nap-FF was chosen as the basis for subsequent connection to sulfanilamide.

Then, after the NapFF-sulfanilamide hydrogel was fabricated, the results of gelation and rheological tests are shown in Fig. S4. Both Nap-FF and NapFF-sulfanilamide are gel-like, but the strength of NapFF-sulfanilamide is significantly higher, and it can form hydrogels from beginning to end in rheological measurements. Therefore, we constructed a more stable hydrogel-based system for subsequent detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric variations triggered by nitrite with traditional griess reagents
ce:section.ce:para: As seen from Fig. 2 A, through the detection of nitrite with Griess reagents, the color gradually became darker as the concentration increased from 0 to 0.3 mM, showing a significant colorimetric variation, while the color at a high concentration (above 0.8 mM) remained the same.

In addition, the value of ∆E * ab is plotted in Fig. 2B. L1 * , a1 * , and b1 * at a concentration of 0 mM were set as the standard values and different nitrite concentrations were detected. Similarly, it can be observed from the figure that ∆E * ab shows an excellent linear correlation (0.98701) in the range of 0–0.3 mM, and the total color difference value (∆E * ab) at concentrations greater than 0.8 mM is approximately without much difference in color. This result is consistent with the visual observations shown in photographs. As indicated in Table 1, the limit of detection (LOD) for nitrite was 17.8 μM (n ≥ 8) with the traditional Griess assay, and it was calculated with the formula LOD = 3 * σ/S (where σ is the standard deviation of the blank, and S is the slope of the calibration plot; this shows that this colorimetric mechanism can detect nitrite at levels in the μM range). The relative standard deviation (RSD) for the traditional Griess assay is approximately 1.78%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NapFF-sulfanilamide hydrogel colorimetric variations triggered by nitrite
ce:section.ce:para: Under these Griess-based conditions, a hydrogel-based NapFF-sulfanilamide system was successfully applied for colorimetric detection of NO2 -. As we can see in Fig. 3 A, the color gradually changed from transparent to yellow, as seen in the left half of the photograph, and a slight color change was observed in the right half of the photograph. Likewise, ∆E*ab was calculated using the above color difference formula, and (L*, a*, b*) at a nitrite concentration of 0 mM was set as the standard value. The increase in ∆E*ab also indicated that the color gradually changed from transparent to yellow in the range of 0–1.7 mM and showed a linear correlation (0.98096).

Compared with the abovementioned liquid-phase mechanism, due to the hydrogel structure, the dye molecules were evenly dispersed in 3D space. Accordingly, the colorimetric variation of the hydrogel system was more extensive (to 1.7 mM). Furthermore, in the concentration range of 1.3–16.7 mM, the value of a* also showed a linear trend with increasing nitrite concentration with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.96926. This shows that our sensor is more competitive commercially. Since the dye particles could be uniformly distributed in the 3D hydrogel without precipitation, the particle density increased, and the brightness (L*) decreased progressively. Hence, visually, the color transformed from yellow to brown and gradually became darker. A significant difference in ∆E*ab was not observed because the total color difference of others at high concentrations was similar (by comparison with the nitrite concentration of 0 mM). However, the value of a* exhibited a proportional trend. The overall color gradually turned brown, which indicated that the red tone became more abundant, so the value of a* increased.

The detection limit of nitrite shown in Fig. 3B for our hydrogel-based chemical sensor was calculated to be 0.72 μM (n ≥ 8) based on ∆E * ab. In comparison with the traditional liquid system, the LOD of our hydrogel-based chemical sensor, as shown in Table 1, was relatively low. It was speculated that the dye particles are evenly distributed in the hydrogel without precipitation, so the color variations can be measured more accurately. This makes the sensor more accurate in overlapping concentration areas. In addition, the relative standard deviation for our hydrogel-based chemical sensor was about 0.21%. Our proposed sensing method demonstrated higher precision than the conventional Griess assay. Overall, these results illustrated that this hydrogel-based chemosensor has great potential to be applied for visual and colorimetric detection of NO2 - ions. Additionally, compared to other studies on nitrite ion detection, which are shown in Table 1, our chemosensor proved to be successful owing to its low LOD and wide detection range. Therefore, our chemosensor is very beneficial and superior for detecting NO2 - in aqueous media with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.72 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gelation test of chemosensors with different nitrite concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: After the determination of nitrite with our novel hydrogel-based chemical sensor, as seen in Fig. 4, the structure and strength of the hydrogel could still be maintained. The concentration of nitrite from top to bottom and from left to right is in the following order: 16.7 mM, 10 mM, 5 mM, 3.3 mM, 1.7 mM, 0.8 mM, 0.3 mM, 0.17 mM, 66.7 μM, 33.3 μM, 16.7 μM, 8.3 μM, and 0 μM. Even after the detection reaction was carried out, the gel properties did not change. Especially at high nitrite concentrations where the color is noticeable, the strength of the hydrogel is significantly apparent. The other parts without color all contain excess liquid, not the hydrogel itself. As a consequence, the results confirmed that our hydrogel-based chemosensor remained in a strong hydrogelated state after visual detection. This is a great advantage for the development and application of chemosensors in the environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability analysis of chemosensors with or without nitrite detection
ce:section.ce:para: In prior studies of supermolecular hydrogels, long-term stability was one of the major issues for sensor systems in practical applications [50]. Long-term stability was checked with 1-month stored samples, including NapFF-sulfanilamide hydrogel and NapFF-sulfanilamide hydrogel after nitrite detection, namely, hydrogel-based azo compounds. As shown in Fig. S5, after storing NapFF-sulfanilamide hydrogel for one week, the strength increased steadily over 500 Pa. Because the measurement leads to external force and damage, the next few weeks of testing reduced the strength of the hydrogel, but the strength was still maintained above 100 Pa. Even if the strength was not as high as the initial period, it could still confirm that the long-term stability of the hydrogel was favorable enough.

Correspondingly, after the NO2 - detection of our hydrogel-based chemosensor at concentrations of 8 μM and 17 μM, the results are shown in Fig. S6. In general, the strength of chemosensors after nitrite detection was lower than that before the detection response, but it was maintained to a certain extent (approximately 150 Pa). At concentrations of 8 μM or 17 μM, the initial strength was greater. However, with the external force of testing, the hydrogel strength regularly decreased week by week. After 1 month of monitoring, the final strength of both samples reached approximately 50 Pa, showing that the storage conditions of the chemosensors after detection were also quite promising.

The colorimetric inspections of the proposed detection system toward NO2 - were conducted in the presence of other environmentally relevant cations as well as anions. The effect of these possible interferents was studied at a concentration of 2 mM, and the concentration of nitrite was also the same. As shown in Fig. 5A, these components hardly had distinct effects on NapFF-sulfanilamide hydrogel except for nitrite ions. In other words, only the chemosensor with nitrite turned yellow, and the others did not change color at all. Apart from observing this result with the naked eye, quantification of the color difference was performed as well. As shown in Fig. 5B, the ΔE* for nitrite was set as 1 and the ΔE* values for other ions were compared to it. This showed that there were no significant color differences for other ions based the ratio of ΔE* . These results revealed that our hydrogel-based chemosensor showed excellent selectivity toward nitrite ions, even in the presence of potentially competing ions; therefore, it can be used for effective detection of NO2 - in samples from different origins.

To verify the feasibility of using our sensor under different environmental conditions, NO2 - detection was investigated with three water samples, including deionized water, tap water and lake water from National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University. The real samples were spiked with NO2 - at certain concentrations (10 μM, 50 μM and 100 μM) and pretreated as described above. As shown in Table 2, our sensor displayed excellent precision and reproducibility, as illustrated by the coeﬃcients of variation (≤ 0.51%) and recovery rates (98.37–103.32%). Therefore, these results indicated that the assay we have proposed in this study is very appropriate for detection of NO2 - in practical applications designed to control environmental pollution.

In summary, based on the Griess reaction between NO2 - and NapFF-sulfanilamide, we fabricated a novel and real-time colorimetric detection system. This chemosensor combines a superior hydrogel with sulfanilamide. In addition, our proposed method exhibited various advantages, such as uniform distribution in 3D space, moisture content, and greater portability and higher stability than the traditional liquid-based Griess test. We used a variety of analytical chemistry techniques to ensure the successful synthesis of samples and confirm their detection effects. The results confirmed that after interacting with NO2 -, NapFF-sulfanilamide visually displayed significant color changes from transparent to yellow/orange. Additionally, a linear relationship was observed between the NO2 - concentration and the color difference value (ΔE*), with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.72 μM. Moreover, our colorimetric chemosensor could not only detect a wide linear concentration range from 8.3 μM to 16.7 mM nitrite ions in aqueous media but also maintained high strength in the hydrogelated state. In addition, our proposed sensing method demonstrated higher precision (RSD=0.21%) than the conventional Griess assay (RSD=1.78%). In terms of long-term stability, we concluded that our sensors are guaranteed for at least one month and that the developed color was stable for more than one month. Even in the presence of other competing ions, our sensor exhibited extremely high sensitivity and selectivity toward nitrite ions. Overall, with a fast response time, remarkable selectivity, and high sensitivity, our hydrogel-based colorimetric chemosensor shows promising for detection and quantification of nitrite under environmental conditions.

Yu-Ting Tai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing − original draft. Chia-Yun Cheng: Methodology, Investigation, Writing − original draft. Yen-Shuo Chen: Resources, Funding acquisition. Fu-Hsiang Ko: Supervision, Project administration, Writing − review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan for ﬁnancially supporting this research under contracts MOST 111–2113-M–A49–031 and MOST 110–2119-M-002–018 -MBK. This work is also supported by the Higher Education Sprout Project of the National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University and Ministry of Education, Taiwan.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132298.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.S. Menyo, C.J. Hawker, J.H. Waite, Versatile tuning of supramolecular hydrogels through metal complexation of oxidation-resistant catechol-inspired ligands, Soft Matter. 9 (2013) 10314–10323.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yu-Ting Tai is currently a doctoral student at National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University, Taiwan. She completed her master’s degree in the Department of Materials Science and Engineering at National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University, Taiwan, in 2018 and began her Ph.D. in the same department after her master’s degree. Her research focuses on synthesizing hydrogel-based aptasensors to detect tumor necrosis factor alpha for early chronic disease diagnosis.

Chia-Yun Cheng completed her master’s degree at National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University, Taiwan, in 2020, with majors in materials science and engineering. The subject of her research was the development of chemosensors applied in the environment.

Yen-Shuo Chen is a doctoral student at National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University, Taiwan. He completed his master’s degree in the Department of Electronic Engineering at National Chin-Yi University of Technology, Taiwan in 2018. Currently, he joins an international project with Dr. Ching-Chang Lin from The University of Tokyo. His research focuses on biosensors and perovskite material development.

Fu-Hsiang Ko received his B.S. and M.S. degrees in chemistry from National Tsing Hua University and National Taiwan Normal University in 1989 and 1991, respectively. Then, he obtained a Ph.D. in atomic science (now the Department of Biomedical Engineering and Environmental Sciences) from National Tsing Hua University in 1996. He joined the National Nano Device Lab (now the Taiwan Semiconductor Research Institute, TSRI) as an associate researcher in October 1996 and became researcher and manager in 2002. He moved to the Institute of Nanotechnology, National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University as an associate professor starting in 2005 and was promoted to a full professor on August 1, 2007. He has published more than 200 peer-reviewed journal articles. His research interests include nanomaterials for biosensors, and hybrid nanomaterials with innovative properties and applications.
